title: A charge qubit on solid neon in a spin-qubit compatible circuit QED platform
authors: J. Wang, Y. Tian, I. Grytsenko, A. Jennings, X. Zhou, H. Terai, D. Jin, E. Kawakami
abstract: Electrons floating in vacuum provide a clean platform for quantum information processing due to their isolation from material defects; electrons on solid neon have emerged as a promising qubit platform for its long coherence times. Here, toward spin-qubit realization, we couple a single electron on solid neon to a magnetic-field-compatible superconducting NbTiN nanowire resonator. We realize a charge qubit and demonstrate microwave readout and coherent control, with Rabi frequencies up to 76 MHz, an order of magnitude larger than in previous studies. Under strong driving, we observe a qubit frequency shift from nonlinear interactions with the intense microwave field. Deterministic electron trapping at an intended position remains challenging due to solid neon surface roughness; we characterize the electron's position from its differential coupling to distinct electrodes. Although not trapped at an intended position, our estimates indicate that spin-qubit demonstrations remain feasible.
summary: This paper reports on the experimental realization and characterization of a charge qubit using an electron trapped on solid neon coupled to a superconducting resonator. The researchers demonstrated coherent control, measured high Rabi frequencies, and analyzed frequency shifts caused by strong microwave driving. The results strongly suggest that this platform is viable for future spin-qubit quantum computation.
main_problem: The research aims to demonstrate and characterize the coherent control of a charge qubit realized using an electron trapped on solid neon, with the ultimate goal of realizing a functional spin-qubit platform.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated microwave readout and coherent control, achieving Rabi frequencies up to 76 MHz and estimating that spin-qubit fidelities approaching 99.5% are feasible.
method: The study employs microwave spectroscopy, Rabi oscillation measurements, and coherence time characterization (Hahn-echo) on a superconducting circuit QED platform.
model_or_system: The system consists of a single electron confined in a double-well potential on solid neon, coupled to a superconducting NbTiN nanowire resonator. The qubit Hamiltonian describes the orbital energy splitting, and the coupling allows for state readout via resonator frequency shifts.
key_observables: Rabi frequencies (up to 76 MHz), qubit frequency shifts (due to nonlinear/ponderomotive effects), coherence times ($T_2^*, T_2$), and single-qubit gate fidelities.
important_parameters: Coupling strength ($g \approx 2.1 	ext{ MHz}$), resonator frequency ($	ext{fr} \approx 5.3 	ext{ GHz}$), and the achievable fidelity ($\approx 99.5\%$).
main_assumptions: Deterministic electron trapping is difficult due to surface roughness, and the analysis relies on fitting resonator response to infer electron position and coupling parameters.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate device structures, potential energy diagrams (double-well), Rabi oscillation measurements vs. drive strength, and frequency shifts attributed to dispersive and ponderomotive effects.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from demonstrating basic charge qubit control and readout, to characterizing coherence times, analyzing frequency shifts under strong driving (dispersive/ponderomotive effects), and finally projecting these results onto the feasibility and fidelity of a spin-qubit realization.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the experimental realization of solid-state qubits, combining concepts from circuit QED, solid-state physics, and quantum control, making it highly relevant for AMO and quantum computing researchers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (A) Optical micrograph of the NbTiN nanowire resonator (false-color pink). The resonator is capacitively and inductively coupled to a feed line (bottom), through which microwave transmission is measured. The dotted square indicates the electron trapping region (enlarged in panel B). (B) Scanning electron micrograph of the electron trapping region, where the two ends of the nanowire resonator center conductor (highlighted in pink) approach each other with a 100 nm separation, with trapping electrodes G1–G4 located nearby. The light blue sphere denotes the electron position inferred from the experimental results (enlarged in panel C). Inset: Expanded view around the originally...

FIG. 2. (A) Schematic of the Rabi measurement sequence. A drive pulse at frequency fd with length td is followed by a probe pulse at frequency fp with a length of 2 µs. Each data point is averaged over N = 300 measurements with a waiting time of 1 ms between repetitions. The drive strength is set to A/A0 = 0.3. (B) Transmission amplitude and calibrated phase of the probe signal as a function of the probe frequency fp around fr for the electron in states |0⟩(blue, no drive pulse) and |1⟩(red, with a π drive pulse), showing the dispersive shift of the resonator frequency of 63 kHz depending on the qubit state. (C) Rabi oscillations of the qubit measured by sweeping the drive frequency fd...

FIG. 3. (A) Schematic of the Ramsey measurement sequence. Two π/2 pulses are applied with a waiting time tw between them and a relative phase difference ∆Φ. A probe pulse with a pulse duration of 1 µs is applied a few hundred nanoseconds after the second π/2 pulse. Each data point is averaged over N = 100 ∼1000 measurements with a waiting time of 1 ms between repetitions. (B) Ramsey decay at ∆Φ = 0. The solid line shows a fit to exp [ −(tw/T ∗ 2 )β] , yielding β = 1.4±0.4 and T ∗ 2 = 91 ± 23 ns. (C) Ramsey fringes measured by varying the drive frequency fd and the waiting time tw between the two π/2 pulses (D,E) Demonstration of π/2 rotations around an arbitrary axis in the xy plane of the...

FIG. 4. Strong drive regime. The pulse sequence used here is the same as that shown in Fig. 2A. (A) Rabi oscillations for varying drive strength A/A0. The Rabi-chevron pattern of the resonance (referred to as the main peak) is clearly vis- ible. With increasing drive strength, this resonance shifts to- ward lower frequencies, indicating a reduction of the qubit fre- quency fq. A side peak also emerges on the higher-frequency side. Vertical dashed red and blue lines indicate the cen- ter frequencies of the main and side peaks, respectively. (B) Rabi frequency of the main peak as a function of the drive strength; the solid line shows a linear fit. The uncertainty in the Rabi frequency...

FIG. 5. (A) Hahn-echo measurement. The decay is fitted with a single-exponential form exp(−t/T2), yielding T2 = 202 ± 5 ns. (B) Energy relaxation measurement. The solid line is a fit to exp(−tdelay/T1), yielding T1 = 3.056±0.015 µs. (C) Temperature dependence of the relaxation time T1, fit- ted by T1(T) = T1(0) tanh[hfq/(2kBT)]. (D) Energy relax- ation measurement after annealing the solid neon by warming it to 8.6 K, cooling back to below 10 mK, and redepositing electrons. The measured qubit here corresponds to a differ- ent single-electron realization from those used elsewhere in this work. The solid line is a fit to exp(−tdelay/T1), yielding T1 = 17.7 ± 0.6 µs.
